text: Nobody touches the shepherd's pie till I've been in there. Don't bust my crust, baby.
speaker: Keith Richards
context: The Rolling Stones guitarist explaining his strict on-the-road rule about his pre-show shepherd's pie, written in his autobiography 'Life'. Oddly specific and very funny rock-star demand.

source.title: Don't mess with Keith Richards' pre-show shepherd's pie ritual, or he might threaten to cut you up and bake you into one

text: I'm playing the same flute, but every nationality of driver will turn around and tell me it reminds them of their country.
speaker: André 3000
context: The Outkast rapper, on Questlove's 'Questlove Supreme' podcast, describing how he'd play his flute in the back of Ubers and how drivers of every nationality told him it reminded them of their home country

source.title: How Did André 3000 Learn To Play Flute?

text: She was flamboyant. She had bright red lipstick, long red fingernails. She had high-heeled shoes, little floating plastic goldfish in the heels of them, short skirts, low-cut tops, and I just thought she was beautiful.
speaker: Dolly Parton
context: In a Guardian interview (quoted by HuffPost), Dolly Parton describes the local 'town tramp' whose flashy style inspired her own signature look as a child.

source.title: Salon — Dolly Parton's famed "town tramp" style inspiration

text: No. I have no technical ability. And I know nothing about music.
speaker: Rick Rubin
context: Super-producer Rick Rubin (Def Jam co-founder; produced Johnny Cash, Beastie Boys, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele, Jay-Z) when Anderson Cooper asked on 60 Minutes whether he knew how to work a soundboard.

source.title: Rick Rubin: The 60 Minutes Interview (transcript) — interview by Anderson Cooper, CBS 60 Minutes

text: I have a credit on the back of the album that says, 'Nails by Dolly!'
speaker: Dolly Parton
context: Dolly Parton explaining that she composed and partly performed the percussive 'typewriter' rhythm of '9 to 5' by clacking her long acrylic fingernails together — and that the album '9 to 5 and Odd Jobs' literally credits 'Nails by Dolly.'

source.title: “I have a credit on the back of the album that says, ‘Nails by Dolly!’” Dolly Parton on the hit song she wrote without a

text: A genius is the one most like himself.
speaker: Thelonious Monk
context: One line from 'T. Monk's Advice' — a set of pointers the jazz pianist Thelonious Monk gave saxophonist Steve Lacy, who wrote them down while playing in Monk's band in 1960.

source.title: Thelonious Monk’s Advice — Howard Rees’ Jazz Workshops (transcribed by Steve Lacy, 1960)

text: My topic is the shift from 'architect' to 'gardener', where 'architect' stands for 'someone who carries a full picture of the work before it is made', to 'gardener' standing for 'someone who plants seeds and waits to see exactly what will come up'.
speaker: Brian Eno
context: Composer/producer Brian Eno (Roxy Music, ambient music pioneer, producer for Bowie, Talking Heads, U2) framing how he thinks about making music — not as an architect with the finished work in his head, but as a gardener who plants seeds

source.title: Composers as Gardeners — Brian Eno, Edge.org

text: Why am I crying on Oprah?
speaker: Pharrell Williams
context: On Oprah Winfrey's talk show 'Oprah Prime' (April 2014), Oprah surprised Pharrell by playing a montage of fan videos of people around the world dancing to his hit song 'Happy.'

source.title: Watch Pharrell Cry Over 'Happy' Inspiration During Interview with Oprah Winfrey

exempt_reason: genuine, well-attested quote (Oprah Prime, April 2014, widely reported incl. Rolling Stone) but too short to pass quote_is_verbatim's 25-char minimum-length floor — a structural limitation of the verbatim algorithm, not a fabrication risk

text: Y'all can't read this, can you?
speaker: Stevie Wonder
context: Presenting Song of the Year at the 2016 Grammy Awards (alongside Pentatonix), the blind musician held up the winner's envelope, which was written in Braille, and playfully teased his fellow presenters and the audience.

source.title: Stevie Wonder Charms GRAMMYs Audience With Braille Joke, Important Message

text: I can't possibly accept this award.
speaker: Adele
context: Accepting Album of the Year for '25' at the 2017 Grammy Awards, Adele stunned the room by opening her acceptance speech declaring she couldn't accept the award, then turning to praise Beyonce — whose 'Lemonade' she felt should have won.

source.title: GRAMMY Rewind: Adele Urges That Beyonce's 'Monumental' 'Lemonade' Should've Won Album Of The Year In 2017

text: It's not about how many times you get rejected ... it's about how many times you stand up and are brave and you keep on going.
speaker: Lady Gaga
context: Accepting the Oscar for Best Original Song ('Shallow' from 'A Star Is Born') at the 2019 Academy Awards, Lady Gaga delivered an emotional, encouraging acceptance speech about perseverance and not giving up on your dreams.

source.title: Lady Gaga's 2019 Oscars Acceptance Speech: "If You Have A Dream, Fight For It"

text: He built this entire empire based on what he thought would turn the world on. That was his superpower
speaker: Rob Thomas
context: Remembering Clive Davis

source.title: Music — Rob Thomas Remembers Clive Davis: ‘Losing Clive Is the End of an Era’

text: You're about to play 'Shakedown Street,' and you just did a heroic dose of mushrooms. How do you even perform?
speaker: Susan Tedeschi
context: Recalling touring with Bob Weir and the Other Ones in 2002

source.title: Music — Susan Tedeschi Says Bob Weir Loved to Offer Her Mushrooms

text: When I was really bad, I was not all bad. When I was really trying to be good, I could never be all good. There would be that black streak going through.
speaker: Johnny Cash
context: Reflecting at the close of his 1994 Rolling Stone interview on the two dogs (named Sin and Redemption) on his American Recordings album cover, and his own inner duality.

source.title: Rolling Stone — Johnny Cash: The Rolling Stone Interview

text: We are part of each other, you know. When one person is hurt, it hurts me, too. When I see their condition, I know what they feel, and I feel it. And it hurts.
speaker: B.B. King
context: Explaining why the blues transcends stereotypes and speaks to shared human suffering, in collected words of wisdom published by Guitar Player.

source.title: Guitar Player — Some Collected Words of Wisdom From B.B. King Himself

text: He had the right attitude. He chewed gum, looked arrogant and flipped the bird to the guards – he did everything the prisoners wanted to do. He was a mean mother from the South who was there because he loved us. When he walked away, everyone in that place had become a Johnny Cash fan.
speaker: Merle Haggard
context: Recalling watching Johnny Cash perform at San Quentin prison on New Year's Day while Haggard was an inmate there.

source.title: Louder — Johnny Cash, Merle Haggard, and the true story that's almost too good to be true

text: The song is the power; the singer is the messenger. The greatest singer in the world cannot save a bad song.
speaker: Quincy Jones
context: On what he considers the most important lesson he learned as a producer, in his widely-shared 2018 Vulture interview.

source.title: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution — 9 of the best quotes from Quincy Jones' sweeping Vulture interview

text: I really feel like some angels have been carrying me around.
speaker: Bonnie Raitt
context: On feeling happier than ever after getting sober, in her Rolling Stone interview following her Grammy sweep for 'Nick of Time.'

source.title: Rolling Stone — Bonnie Raitt: The Rolling Stone Interview

text: A rhinestone shines just as good as a diamond.
speaker: Dolly Parton
context: On being 'totally artificial' in appearance yet 'totally real' as an artist and person, in her November 2019 ELLE interview.

source.title: Yahoo Lifestyle — 20 Refreshingly Honest Dolly Parton Quotes

text: 'Bull riding and boobies' — that is potentially the greatest lyric in any song ever.
speaker: Harry Styles
context: In his 2019 Rolling Stone cover interview, riffing on a line from Lil Nas X's 'Old Town Road.'

source.title: Rolling Stone — The Eternal Sunshine of Harry Styles

text: I am one of the funniest people alive, people have to understand that
speaker: Vince Staples
context: In a 2015 Howl & Echoes interview, responding to praise about his deadpan tweets.

source.title: Howl & Echoes — INTERVIEW: Vince Staples - "I am one of the funniest people alive, people have to understand that"

text: I think, actually, I'd be more successful if I was OK wearing a muzzle.
speaker: Chappell Roan
context: In a January 2025 BBC interview after being named BBC Sound of 2025, on how her outspokenness affects her career.

source.title: The Hollywood Reporter — Chappell Roan: "I'd Be More Successful If I Was OK Wearing a Muzzle"

text: Gluttony and lust – y'know, Caligula vibes! What else is there? I've been slothful, definitely. I've ticked the wrath, greed and heresy boxes too.
speaker: Hozier
context: In a Hotpress interview about his concept album Unreal Unearth, asked which of the seven deadly sins were the most fun to commit.

source.title: Hotpress — 12 Interviews of Xmas: Hozier on Irishness, Deadly Sins, Joni Mitchell and Unreal Unearth

text: A great song can make the worst singer in the world a star. And a bad song can't be saved the three greatest singers in the world.
speaker: Quincy Jones
context: Reflecting on the primacy of the song itself, in a Rolling Stone interview about producing Michael Jackson's album Bad.

source.title: Rolling Stone — Quincy Jones Looks Back on the Making of Michael Jackson's 'Bad'

text: You might be ingraining into your practise something that is counter productive and you'd have no way of knowing it.
speaker: Steve Albini
context: Warning aspiring engineers why doing 'whatever random crap you can find' is a trap, in an Audio Media International interview on how to be a better producer.

source.title: Audio Media International — 'You might be doing a bad job and not know it': Steve Albini on how to be a better producer

text: It's a lot like being a chef, in a way. You're trying to create food that satisfies your taste, but with a good awareness that there are people whose tastes are for more salt, or more spice — or for less.
speaker: Susan Rogers
context: Prince's former recording engineer describing the producer's balancing act, in a Grammy.com interview about her career and her book.

source.title: Grammy.com — Producer & Engineer Susan Rogers Worked With Prince And Barenaked Ladies

text: Keep your mind on the music. I never thought of myself as a 'woman' musician at all, but a guitarist and then a bass player
speaker: Carol Kaye
context: The legendary session bassist (Pet Sounds, countless hits) on identity versus playing, in a Guitar World interview.

source.title: Guitar World — Studio bass icon Carol Kaye on why it is not about who you are, but how and what you play

text: When I was young I studied classical music and Giuseppe Verdi has this wonderful quote: 'Good composers borrow, great composers steal.'
speaker: Nile Rodgers
context: The Chic guitarist and producer on how he views artists who borrow from his work, in an interview with The National.

source.title: The National — With Nile Rodgers, it is always bound to be Good Times

text: The music always precedes the words. The words often come from the sound of the music and eventually evolve into coherent thoughts. Or incoherent thoughts.
speaker: Paul Simon
context: Describing his music-first songwriting process in an American Songwriter interview tied to his Songwriter collection.

source.title: American Songwriter — Interview: Paul Simon Discusses Songwriter And Songwriting

text: I can out-dance Bowie and Jagger!
speaker: Martha Reeves
context: An interview about her career and Motown

source.title: Music — ‘I can out-dance Bowie and Jagger!’ Martha Reeves on Motown, Dancing in the Stre
